nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; commonly observed in rocky reef areas near the shore, generally reported from about 3 to 30 m depth, with occasional records down to 50 m. Adults are typically encountered in pairs or small groups; the species also enters mangrove and estuarine fringes along the eastern Pacific coast.

diet.notes: In the wild, this species has been documented feeding on a mixed diet of algae and benthic invertebrates, which gives it a somewhat broader and more flexible palate than many obligate-corallivore butterflyfishes. In captivity, individuals are generally reported to acclimate well to a varied diet of meaty frozen foods (mysis, enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood) alongside algae- and spirulina-based preparations and vitamin-enriched marine flakes or pellets. Several small feedings per day are commonly advised, and live foods such as black worms can be useful to trigger initial feeding in newly acquired specimens.

imageCandidates.notes: Dan Schofield; 2048x1536. Sharp in-situ reef shot, whole fish framed against pink coral; three bold dark vertical bars, silvery scaled body and eye stripe all clearly shown. Best diagnostic pick.
Paul and Jill; 6000x4000. Single fish in natural rocky-reef habitat showing the three bars and yellow-edged caudal fin; slightly soft/distant so kept as non-recommended.
Thefishingnomad; 4000x3000. Very sharp full-body reference clearly showing three bars, dorsal spines and yellow anal/caudal fins; caveat: held out of water in hand.

summary: The Threebanded Butterflyfish (Chaetodon humeralis) is an Eastern Pacific species marked by three dark vertical bars across a silvery body and a yellow-edged caudal fin. Generally considered peaceful and reasonably hardy, it is not reef-safe and is best housed in fish-only or FOWLR systems.
careNotes:
The Threebanded Butterflyfish is the only common butterflyfish from the Eastern Pacific, ranging from San Diego, California south to Peru and including the Gulf of California and the Galápagos Islands. Compared to most reef butterflyfishes, it is considered relatively hardy and is generally reported to accept prepared foods more readily than obligate corallivores (species that feed exclusively on living coral tissue). Together these traits make it a reasonable intermediate-level choice for keepers of peaceful fish-only or FOWLR (fish-only with live rock) systems.

The species is not generally considered reef-safe: it has been reported to nip soft corals and large polyp stony corals (LPS), graze tubeworms, and pick at the mantles of Tridacna and other clams. For these reasons it is generally advised against mixed-reef aquaria. A FOWLR setup with plenty of live rock for grazing, dispersed rockwork, and substantial open swimming space is the more reliable choice. Adults are typically encountered in pairs or small groups in the wild, and the species is widely reported to form monogamous breeding pairs; established pairs should be purchased and kept together rather than split.

A minimum aquarium volume of around 500 L is generally advised to accommodate the species' active swimming habits and need for an open footprint. Hobby temperature recommendations vary across sources, and wild ocean records span 17.3–28.9°C; a range of 22–26°C represents a practical midpoint that should suit most home systems.

Captive breeding has not been documented and is generally considered impractical in home aquariums.
breedingNotes: Reported to be oviparous and to form monogamous pairs during the breeding season in the wild. Captive breeding has not been documented in home aquariums and is generally considered impractical.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary care reference: confirmed scientific name Chaetodon humeralis, family Chaetodontidae, peaceful temperament, moderate care, omnivorous diet (algae and benthic invertebrates), 120 US gal minimum, 6.5 in maximum size, temperature 68–76°F (20–24°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, not reef safe.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Chaetodontidae / Acanthuriformes), Eastern Pacific range (San Diego to Peru, including the Galápagos), depth 3–50 m (typically 3–12 m), wild temperature 17.3–28.9°C (mean 25.2°C), adult diet of algae and benthic invertebrates, oviparous monogamous pair-spawning, IUCN Least Concern. FishBase reports max 25.4 cm TL (common length 12 cm), which is larger than LiveAquaria's 6.5 in figure; values were averaged toward the hobby figure with the wild maximum documented in careNotes.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Cross-checked peaceful temperament, hardy/intermediate care, not reef safe (eats tubeworms, nibbles Tridacna clams, eats soft corals and LPS), mated-pair grouping, Eastern Pacific range, decor preferences (live rock with ample swimming space). ReefApp lists max 25 cm and minimum 600 L; the 500 L tank-size figure used here is between the LiveAquaria (120 gal ≈ 454 L) and ReefApp (600 L) recommendations.

additional.name: Saltwaterfish.com
additional.notes: Cross-checked 6–8 in adult size, 120 US gal minimum tank, temperature 74–82°F (23–28°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.022–1.025, peaceful temperament, moderate care, not reef safe, 5–10 year lifespan.

additional.name: ReefGuide.org
additional.notes: Field-guide reference; confirmed Gulf of California / Pacific Coast of Mexico to Peru range and 3–30 m depth. Size figure of 12 cm here likely reflects common (not maximum) length.

fish.breedingNotes: The species is oviparous (egg-scattering) and is reported to form monogamous pairs during the breeding season in the wild. Captive breeding has not been documented in home aquariums.
